title: Condition scoring sheep
subtitle: Using the standard 0 to 5 scale to assess body fat and muscle in a flock.
excerpt: Condition scoring tells you whether each ewe has enough body reserves for the stage of production she is in. A ewe going into tupping at a score of 2 or below will have a lower conception rate than one at 3; a ewe lambing at a score of 4 risks ketosis. This covers the manual technique and what each score means in practice.
sourceNotes: AHDB Beef and Lamb, Body Condition Scoring in Sheep (published guidance). SAC Consulting Scotland, flock management factsheets. DEFRA Welfare Code for Sheep (2023).

content.text: Condition scoring is done by hand, not by eye. A fleece conceals body changes; the hand over the
drives feeding decisions for the ewe at every stage from weaning through tupping, pregnancy, and lactation.

Catch and hold the ewe with one hand on the back of the jaw and the other hand free to palpate. Place your hand flat across the spine directly behind the last rib and press down gently. You are feeling two things: the sharpness of the spine (the spinous processes) and the fat cover over the short ribs (the transverse processes).
Score 1: Spine very sharp and prominent; transverse processes sharp and easy to press under. No fat cover felt. Animal is very thin.
Score 2: Spine prominent but smooth; transverse processes feel rounded and can be pressed under with slight pressure. Some fat cover.
Score 3: Spine detectable only as a line; transverse processes smooth and well covered; can be felt with firm pressure. Adequate for most production stages.
Score 4: Spine detectable only with hard pressure; transverse processes cannot be individually felt. Getting fat.
Score 5: Spine not detectable; no individual transverse processes felt. Obese. Ewes at this score are at risk of pregnancy toxaemia and lambing difficulties.

Target 3 to 3.5 at tupping for ewe lambs and first-season ewes; 3 to 4 for mature ewes. Drop to 2.5 to 3 going into late pregnancy (over-fat ewes have more lambing problems). Ewes reaching lambing below 2 are at high risk of
and need supplementary feeding with energy concentrate from eight weeks before lambing.
